preamble.summary: A thunderstorm psalm — the voice of the LORD crashes seven times across the landscape, shattering cedars, splitting flames, shaking the wilderness, stripping forests bare, and causing deer to writhe. The psalm opens with a summons to the heavenly beings to ascribe glory to the LORD, traces the storm's path from the Mediterranean coast eastward through Lebanon and the desert, and closes with the LORD enthroned over the cosmic flood, giving strength and peace to his people.
preamble.remarkable: The sevenfold repetition of qol YHWH ('the voice of the LORD') is the structural backbone of the psalm. Seven is the number of divine completeness, and each occurrence of 'the voice' intensifies the storm: powerful, majestic, breaking cedars, splitting fire, shaking wilderness, causing deer to calve, stripping forests. The storm moves geographically from west to east — from the 'great waters' (Mediterranean, v. 3) through Lebanon and Sirion (v. 6) to the wilderness of Kadesh (v. 8). This is not a storm observed from shelter; it is a theophany — God's self-revelation through raw natural power. Some scholars have noted structural parallels with Ugaritic hymns to Baal the storm god, suggesting that this psalm may deliberately reassign Baal's attributes to YHWH: the God of Israel is the true lord of the storm.
preamble.friction: The phrase benei elim ('sons of the mighty' or 'sons of gods,' v. 1) addresses heavenly beings — members of the divine council — not human worshippers. The psalm's opening audience is not the congregation in the temple but the angelic assembly in heaven. This raises the question of who is 'overhearing' this cosmic worship and why. The answer may be that the psalm invites human worshippers to join a worship service already in progress among the heavenly beings. The 'flood' (mabbul, v. 10) appears only here and in the Genesis flood narrative, linking God's enthronement to his sovereignty over the primordial waters of chaos.
preamble.connections: The 'voice of the LORD' as storm theophany connects to Exodus 19:16-19 (thunder at Sinai), 1 Kings 19:11-12 (the voice that was not in the wind, earthquake, or fire), and Job 37-38 (God speaking from the whirlwind). The cedar imagery links to 2 Kings 19:23 and Isaiah 2:13. The 'flood' (mabbul) of verse 10 connects to Genesis 6-9 and to the cosmic waters of Psalm 93:3-4. The closing benediction of strength (oz) and peace (shalom) anticipates the Aaronic blessing of Numbers 6:24-26. Jesus stilling the storm (Mark 4:35-41) demonstrates authority over the same forces celebrated in this psalm.

verses.text_hebrew: מִזְמ֗וֹר לְדָ֫וִ֥ד הָב֣וּ לַ֭יהוָה בְּנֵ֣י אֵלִ֑ים הָב֥וּ לַ֝יהוָ֗ה כָּב֥וֹד וָעֹֽז׃
verses.text_kjv: Give unto the LORD, O ye mighty, give unto the LORD glory and strength.
verses.rendering:
A psalm of David.
Ascribe to the LORD, O heavenly beings!
Ascribe to the LORD glory and strength!
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm opens not with human worshippers but with benei elim ('sons of the mighty' or 'sons of gods') — the heavenly beings who form God's cosmic court. The verb havu ('give, ascribe, bring') is repeated three times in verses 1-2, creating a threefold summons. The heavenly beings are commanded to ascribe kavod ('glory, weight, honor') and oz ('strength, power') to the LORD. This is not because God lacks glory and needs it from his angels — it is the recognition and declaration of what God already is. The psalm establishes from its first line that worship begins in heaven before it reaches earth.
translator_notes: Benei elim ('sons of the mighty') is linguistically related to benei ha-elohim ('sons of God') in Job 1:6, 2:1, and Genesis 6:2 — members of the heavenly council. The Septuagint translates this as 'sons of God.' The rendering 'heavenly beings' captures the meaning without importing later angelology into the text.

key_terms.semantic_range: glory, honor, weight, heaviness, radiance, splendor, reputation
key_terms.note: Kavod in this context is the visible, weighty manifestation of God's nature that the heavenly beings are commanded to acknowledge. The storm that follows (vv. 3-9) is kavod made audible and visible — God's weight crashing through the physical world.

verses.text_hebrew: הָב֣וּ לַ֭יהוָה כְּב֣וֹד שְׁמ֑וֹ הִשְׁתַּחֲו֥וּ לַ֝יהוָ֗ה בְּהַדְרַת־קֹֽדֶשׁ׃
verses.text_kjv: Give unto the LORD the glory due unto his name; worship the LORD in the beauty of holiness.
verses.rendering:
Ascribe to the LORD the glory of his name;
bow down to the LORD in the splendor of holiness.
translator_notes: Kevod shemo ('the glory of his name') — the name bears the full weight of God's character, and the heavenly beings are to honor it accordingly. Hishtachavu ('bow down, prostrate yourselves') is the most physically complete gesture of worship — full-body prostration. Be-hadrat-qodesh ('in the splendor/adornment of holiness') could describe the worshippers dressed in holy garments or the setting of worship adorned with holiness. Either way, holiness is the environment in which true worship occurs.

verses.text_hebrew: ק֤וֹל יְהוָ֗ה עַל־הַ֫מָּ֥יִם אֵֽל־הַכָּב֥וֹד הִרְעִ֑ים יְ֝הוָ֗ה עַל־מַ֥יִם רַבִּֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: The voice of the LORD is upon the waters: the God of glory thundereth: the LORD is upon many waters.
verses.rendering:
The voice of the LORD is over the waters.
The God of glory thunders —
the LORD, over the great waters.
verses.expanded_rendering: The storm begins over the sea. Qol YHWH al-ha-mayim ('the voice of the LORD over the waters') — the first of seven occurrences of qol YHWH. Thunder is God's voice; the storm is God speaking. El ha-kavod hirim ('the God of glory thunders') — the thunder is not merely weather; it is kavod made audible. The mayim rabbim ('great waters, many waters') are the Mediterranean, or the cosmic waters of primordial chaos, or both. The storm that rolls in from the sea is the voice of the God who subdued the waters at creation, and his voice still controls them.
translator_notes: The connection between thunder and divine speech is ancient and cross-cultural, but in the Hebrew Bible it carries specific theological freight. At Sinai, God spoke from the mountain with thunder and fire (Exodus 19:16-19). Here, the entire landscape becomes Sinai — God speaks from every direction.

verses.text_kjv: The voice of the LORD is powerful; the voice of the LORD is full of majesty.
verses.rendering:
The voice of the LORD is powerful;
the voice of the LORD is majestic.
translator_notes: Two attributes of the divine voice: ba-koach ('in power') and be-hadar ('in majesty, splendor'). The voice is both forceful and beautiful — it does not merely destroy; it overwhelms with grandeur. Koach is raw power; hadar is the aesthetic impact of that power. Together they describe an experience that is simultaneously terrifying and magnificent.

verses.text_hebrew: ק֣וֹל יְ֭הוָה שֹׁבֵ֣ר אֲרָזִ֑ים וַיְשַׁבֵּ֥ר יְ֝הוָ֗ה אֶת־אַרְזֵ֥י הַלְּבָנֽוֹן׃
verses.text_kjv: The voice of the LORD breaketh the cedars; yea, the LORD breaketh the cedars of Lebanon.
verses.rendering:
The voice of the LORD shatters the cedars;
the LORD shatters the cedars of Lebanon.
translator_notes: The cedars of Lebanon (arzei ha-Levanon) were the largest, strongest, most majestic trees in the ancient world — the ancient equivalent of skyscrapers. They were symbols of power, permanence, and human pride (Isaiah 2:13). When God's voice shatters (shover) them, it demonstrates power that dwarfs the most impressive works of nature. If the cedars cannot stand, nothing can.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיַּרְקִידֵ֥ם כְּמוֹ־עֵ֑גֶל לְבָנ֥וֹן וְ֝שִׂרְיֹ֗ן כְּמ֣וֹ בֶן־רְאֵמִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: He maketh them also to skip like a calf; Lebanon and Sirion like a young unicorn.
verses.rendering:
He makes Lebanon skip like a calf,
and Sirion like a young wild ox.
verses.expanded_rendering: The image is intentionally absurd — entire mountain ranges leaping like calves. Lebanon and Sirion (the Sidonian name for Mount Hermon, per Deuteronomy 3:9) are massive, immovable geological formations, and God's voice makes them dance. The verb yarqid ('to make skip, cause to leap') is playful, almost comic — the storm treats mountains the way a shepherd treats young animals. Ben reemim ('a young wild ox') emphasizes both power and playful energy. The mountains that seem permanent and unmovable are playthings before the voice of God.
translator_notes: Sirion is identified with Mount Hermon in Deuteronomy 3:9. The psalm traces the storm's path from the Mediterranean coast (v. 3) through the Lebanon range and Hermon (v. 6) and into the southern wilderness (v. 8), covering the entire north-south extent of the land.

verses.text_kjv: The voice of the LORD divideth the flames of fire.
verses.rendering: The voice of the LORD splits flames of fire.
translator_notes: Chotsev lahavot esh ('hewing/splitting flames of fire') — lightning. The verb chatsav means 'to hew, cut, quarry' — it is used for cutting stone. God's voice cuts fire the way a mason cuts rock. Lightning is presented not as random electrical discharge but as the deliberate craftsmanship of the divine voice shaping fire into bolts.

verses.text_hebrew: ק֣וֹל יְ֭הוָה יָחִ֣יל מִדְבָּ֑ר יָחִ֥יל יְ֝הוָ֗ה מִדְבַּ֥ר קָדֵֽשׁ׃
verses.text_kjv: The voice of the LORD shaketh the wilderness; the LORD shaketh the wilderness of Kadesh.
verses.rendering:
the LORD shakes the wilderness of Kadesh.
The voice of the LORD shakes the wilderness;
translator_notes: The storm has moved south from Lebanon to the midbar Qadesh ('wilderness of Kadesh') — either Kadesh-barnea in the Negev or a northern Kadesh in Syria. The verb chil/yachil ('to writhe, shake, tremble, be in labor') describes the wilderness convulsing as though in labor pains. The uninhabited wasteland — the place with no audience — trembles before God's voice. God does not need spectators to be powerful.

verses.text_hebrew: ק֤וֹל יְהוָ֨ה ׀ יְחוֹלֵ֣ל אַיָּלוֹת֮ וַֽיֶּחֱשֹׂ֢ף יְעָ֫ר֥וֹת וּבְהֵיכָל֑וֹ כֻּ֝לּ֗וֹ אֹמֵ֥ר כָּבֽוֹד׃
verses.text_kjv: The voice of the LORD maketh the hinds to calve, and discovereth the forests: and in his temple doth every one speak of his glory.
verses.rendering:
The voice of the LORD makes the deer writhe in labor
and strips the forests bare.
And in his temple, all cry: "Glory!"
verses.expanded_rendering: The seventh and final qol YHWH brings the climax. Two effects: the deer writhe in premature labor (yecholel ayyalot — the storm's terror induces birth), and the forests are stripped bare (yechesof yearot — the trees are denuded by the wind). Nature is simultaneously creating and destroying under the force of God's voice. Then the scene shifts from the external world to the temple: u-ve-hekhalo kullo omer kavod ('and in his temple, all of it/everyone says: Glory!'). The storm outside corresponds to worship inside. While the physical world convulses under God's voice, the worshippers in the temple respond with one word: kavod — 'Glory!' The word that opened the psalm (v. 1) now fills the temple. The heavenly beings were commanded to ascribe glory; now the entire temple community declares it.
translator_notes: The single word kavod as the temple's response is extraordinarily compressed. Everything the storm displays — power, majesty, dominion over nature — is captured in one word. The brevity is the point: when you have witnessed what God's voice can do, no elaborate speech is needed. One word suffices.

key_terms.semantic_range: glory, honor, weight, heaviness, radiance, splendor, reputation
key_terms.note: Kavod closes the psalm's worship arc. The heavenly beings were commanded to ascribe kavod (v. 1). The God of kavod thundered (v. 3). Now the temple resounds with kavod. The storm is the content; kavod is the word that names what the storm reveals.

verses.text_hebrew: יְ֭הוָה לַמַּבּ֣וּל יָשָׁ֑ב וַיֵּ֥שֶׁב יְ֝הוָ֗ה מֶ֣לֶךְ לְעוֹלָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: The LORD sitteth upon the flood; yea, the LORD sitteth King for ever.
verses.rendering:
The LORD sits enthroned over the flood;
the LORD sits enthroned as king forever.
verses.expanded_rendering: The word mabbul ('flood') appears in only two places in the Hebrew Bible: the Genesis flood narrative and this verse. The connection is deliberate. The LORD who sent the flood — the ultimate act of cosmic power over the waters of chaos — is the same LORD who now sits enthroned (yashav) over those waters permanently. The flood was not a one-time event; it revealed a permanent reality. God reigns over chaos. The verb yashav ('to sit, dwell, be enthroned') is repeated: YHWH la-mabbul yashav ('the LORD over the flood sits') and vayyeshev YHWH melekh le-olam ('the LORD sits as king forever'). The double sitting emphasizes stability — God is not pacing or straining; he is seated, settled, permanently enthroned. Melekh le-olam ('king forever') places no time limit on this sovereignty.
translator_notes: The rarity of mabbul makes this verse unusually weighty. Every Hebrew reader would hear the Genesis flood in this word. The psalm is saying: the God whose voice just shattered cedars and shook the wilderness is the same God who once unmade the world with water and who now reigns over those same waters. His sovereignty is both terrifying and total.

verses.text_hebrew: יְֽהוָ֗ה עֹ֭ז לְעַמּ֣וֹ יִתֵּ֑ן יְהוָ֓ה ׀ יְבָרֵ֖ךְ אֶת־עַמּ֣וֹ בַשָּׁלֽוֹם׃
verses.text_kjv: The LORD will give strength unto his people; the LORD will bless his people with peace.
verses.rendering:
The LORD gives strength to his people;
the LORD blesses his people with peace.
verses.expanded_rendering: After eleven verses of cosmic power — heavenly worship, thunder, shattered cedars, skipping mountains, fire, earthquake, stripped forests — the psalm ends with two quiet gifts: oz ('strength') and shalom ('peace'). The God whose voice terrifies creation gives strength to his people. The God who shakes the wilderness gives his people shalom — completeness, wholeness, well-being, the absence of all disorder. The storm that could destroy is redirected into blessing. The people who worship this God receive not the storm's destruction but the storm's power, transformed into the strength they need and the peace they crave. The psalm's final word is shalom — the storm ends in stillness.
translator_notes: The ending creates a profound theological statement: the same power that breaks cedars blesses people. God's strength (oz) is not diminished when given to his people — it is shared. And the result of receiving the strength of the storm-God is not terror but shalom. This is one of the most concise statements of the paradox of divine power in the Psalter.
